    "type": "text",
    "text": " Good \u2014 that's a clearer spec. Let me think through it carefully because there are some subtle interactions to get right.\n\n## What you're asking for, distilled\n\nA new \"ink\" pigment with these properties:\n\n1. **Renders as dark as possible** \u2014 near-black, maximum contrast against paper\n2. **High paint load by default** \u2014 saturates the cell immediately\n3. **Low water load by default** \u2014 minimal wet field deposited, so it doesn't bleed itself\n4. **Adjustable** \u2014 both loads can be overridden per-stamp\n5. **Crisp when deposited** \u2014 no immediate bleed; sharp letter edges\n6. **Blooms when external water is added** \u2014 when another wet pigment splashes onto ink, the ink advects with the wet field like any other pigment\n\nThe \"dry-then-mask\" workflow you mentioned makes sense: dry the paper aggressively before laying ink so the ink has nothing to bleed into, then mask the inked region so subsequent washes don't disturb it until you choose to.\n\n## Design decisions I need to make\n\n**1. How to make \"dark as possible\" with K-M.**\n\nK-M reflectance from a pigment with K (absorption) and S (scattering) is approximately `1 + K/S - sqrt((K/S)\u00b2 + 2K/S)`. To get near-black:\n- Very high K (absorbs all wavelengths) \u2192 low reflectance\n- Very low S (no light bouncing back) \u2192 low reflectance\n\nThe existing pigments (rose, yellow, blue) have moderate K and high S. For ink I'd want **extremely high K, very low S** \u2014 closest analog is real india ink or sumi ink.\n\nSpecifically: K \u2248 [8, 8, 8] (high absorption across all channels), S \u2248 [0.5, 0.5, 0.5] (low scattering). This gives a near-uniform dark gray that approaches black as concentration rises. The current pigments use K values around 0.5-2 and S values around 1-3.\n\n**2. Where to add the ink in the pigment array.**\n\nTwo options:\n- **A \u2014 Add to PIGMENTS array as index 3.** Clean, just another pigment. `wc.pigment('ink')` works the same way as rose/yellow/blue. The lib already has machinery to handle N pigments (the swatch UI iterates over them, etc.).\n- **B \u2014 Add as a special index like MASK_INDEX.** Like a separate channel with custom handling. More work, easy to mess up.\n\nOption A is the obvious right answer. The PIGMENTS array can grow.\n\n**3. How to handle the \"high paint load, low water load by default\" behavior.**\n\nThe lib currently has global `paintLoadMult` and `waterLoadMult` that apply uniformly to all paint operations. To get per-pigment defaults that override these, I have a few choices:\n\n- **C \u2014 Per-pigment multipliers.** Each pigment in PIGMENTS gets optional `paintLoadMult` and `waterLoadMult` overrides. When painting with that pigment, the per-pigment values are used instead of (or multiplied with) the globals.\n- **D \u2014 Per-stamp override.** Add `paintLoad` and `waterLoad` to the paintAt opts object. Caller supplies them or falls back to globals. The \"ink defaults\" are then a convention enforced at the caller site, not in the lib.\n\nC is more elegant \u2014 the ink \"knows\" about itself. D is more flexible but pushes the responsibility to the caller. I'd do C for the defaults and add D as a per-call override.\n\nBut wait \u2014 the existing PIGMENTS entries don't have per-pigment multipliers. Adding them touches the schema. Let me check:",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Now I understand the structure. Each pigment has `K`, `S`, `density`, `staining`, `granulation`. The lib has 3-pigment hardcoded loops in many places (`const p0 = PIGMENTS[0], p1 = PIGMENTS[1], p2 = PIGMENTS[2]`). That's a real architectural constraint.\n\nThis is a problem. **Adding a 4th pigment to PIGMENTS would require updating every site that loops \"for each of 3 pigments\"** \u2014 which is a lot. Render, advection, edge effects, GPU shaders, swatch UI, all use the hardcoded 3. The hardcoded triplet is baked deep into the codebase.\n\nLet me re-evaluate my plan. Three options:\n\n**Option X \u2014 Replace blue with ink.** Only 3 pigments, but one is \"ink\". You lose blue. Too destructive.\n\n**Option Y \u2014 Use index 0/1/2 dynamically.** Switch active palette like gouacheMode does. There's already PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT vs PIGMENTS_OPAQUE \u2014 could add PIGMENTS_INK with [rose, yellow, ink]. But you can't have ink AND blue simultaneously.\n\n**Option Z \u2014 Add ink as a special index like MASK/WATER.** Ink lives in a 4th channel that exists alongside the 3-pigment array. Render adds ink's contribution on top of the K-M result. Doesn't fight the hardcoded-3 architecture.\n\n**Option Z is the right design.** Let me reconsider \u2014 yes this matches my earlier \"Option B\" framing.\n\nActually, the simplest implementation of Z: **ink lives in its own Float32Array, treated like a pigment for advection/wet-coupling, but at render time it composites separately.** Render pipeline:\n1. Compute K-M reflectance from g[0], g[1], g[2] as usual\n2. Multiply by `(1 - ink[i] * alpha)` to darken in proportion to ink concentration\n3. Optionally add a tiny scattering term so very-dense ink isn't pure black (more realistic)\n\nThis adds one channel and one render-step modification. Doesn't touch the hardcoded 3-pigment loops at all.\n\nFor paint deposition:\n- `paintAt(gx, gy, r, 'ink', strength)` \u2192 deposits into ink[] instead of g[k]\n- Uses configurable ink-specific paint load and water load\n- Same advection: when wet flows, ink advects with it just like pigment\n\nFor advection: the lib's pigment advection moves `g[k]` cells along velocity vectors. I need to do the same for `ink[]`. Look at advection:",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Lots of hardcoded loops. **But for ink, I don't need to add it to those loops** \u2014 ink is a separate channel that:\n- Gets its own advection step (separate function call alongside the existing pigment advection)\n- Gets its own evaporation deposit (or none \u2014 ink is ink, it doesn't get suspended/deposited like pigment)\n- Gets its own render pass that darkens the K-M result\n\nActually let me think simpler. **What does ink actually need to behave like?**\n\nYou want crisp text. You want it dark. You want it to bloom *when external water hits it*. So:\n\n- **Deposit ink** = mark a cell as \"has ink at concentration X\"\n- **Advection** = when wet field at that cell has velocity, the ink moves with the wet. Identical to pigment advection.\n- **No evaporation deposit** = ink doesn't get \"suspended\". It's just always at the cell, period. It can move via advection but it doesn't have a \"wet vs dry\" state distinction like pigment does.\n- **Render** = ink darkens the cell's reflectance in proportion to its concentration\n\nIf ink uses the SAME advection field as pigment (the velocity field driven by wet), then I just need to advect it whenever pigment advects. The cleanest way: make ink a \"fourth pigment\" but only for advection \u2014 extend the hardcoded 3-loops to be 4-loops when ink is active.\n\nThat's actually less invasive than I feared. The pigment loops at lines 1280, 1419, 1568 etc. are just `for k in 0..3`. Changing to `for k in 0..N` where N=3 or 4 depending on ink-active is a few lines.\n\nBut that touches a LOT of sites (10+ loops based on the grep). Let me think of an even simpler approach.\n\n**Cleanest minimal-touch design:** Ink is a separate `Float32Array ink[N]`. Has its own `advectInk()` function called from `simStep` right after `movePigment()`. Reuses the velocity field. Render adds an ink-darkening multiplier on top of the K-M output. New API: `wc.paintInk(gx, gy, gridRadius, paintLoad, waterLoad)`.\n\nThat's:\n1. New array (~7KB at 648\u00d7540)\n2. New advection function (~30 lines, copy-adapt from one of the pigment advection branches)\n3. New paint function (~30 lines)\n4. New render hook (~10 lines)\n5. New API methods\n\nAbout 150 lines total. Doesn't touch any existing 3-pigment loops because ink lives outside them.\n\nThis is the right design. Let me also think about the dry-then-mask workflow you mentioned.\n\n**Dry-then-mask flow for the demo:**\n1. Set evaporation very high (e.g. 200 \u2014 bone-dry)\n2. Wait a frame or two for wet to evaporate\n3. Paint ink (now there's no wet for it to bleed into; sharp deposit)\n4. Mask the ink region\n5. Restore evaporation\n6. Paint surrounding washes (mask protects ink)\n\nStep 2 (waiting) is the tricky one. In the current setup() everything is synchronous so \"wait a frame\" needs an `await new Promise(r => requestAnimationFrame(r))`. That's fine.\n\nBut wait \u2014 ink with `waterLoad: 0` would have minimal effect on wet anyway. If ink doesn't add wet, the dry-paper step is less critical \u2014 the ink itself isn't wet. The dry-paper step is only needed if ink uses some wet, which is the configurable case. By default with waterLoad=0, ink lands on whatever wet was there, and dries immediately.\n\nOK plan:\n1. **Add `ink[]` array** to lib\n2. **Add `advectInk()`** mirroring pigment advection, called after movePigment in simStep\n3. **Add `paintInk()` API** with high paintLoad default (1.0) and low waterLoad default (0.05)\n4. **Add ink render contribution** \u2014 darkens K-M output by `1 - ink[i] * INK_ALPHA`\n5. **Add ink to `removeMask()` / mask checks** \u2014 masked cells reject ink deposits, same as pigment\n6. **Update demo** to use `wc.paintInk()` for the title/body/link text\n\nLet me build.",

Lives outside the 3-pigment K-M system. Renders\n// as a darkening multiplier applied on top of the K-M reflectance.\n// Designed for \"print text on watercolor paper\" use cases: a third\n// medium that has its own crisp deposition profile but bleeds with\n// the wet field like the pigments do.\n//\n// Differences from a pigment:\n//   \u2022 Single channel (one Float32Array) \u2014 no per-RGB K/S because ink\n//     doesn't scatter, it absorbs uniformly across wavelengths.\n//   \u2022 No suspended/deposited split \u2014 ink is just \"amount per cell\".\n//     Doesn't go through the evaporation transfer that pigment uses.\n//   \u2022 Advects with the velocity field same as pigment (when external\n//     wet causes flow, ink moves with it \u2014 that's the \"bloom when\n//     other pigments hit it\" behavior).\n//   \u2022 Default paint load high (saturate immediately for crisp letters);\n//     default water load low (don't deposit wet alongside, so ink\n//     stays sharp by default).\nlet ink = new Float32Array(N);\nlet ink_tmp = new Float32Array(N);\nlet inkActive = false;  // gate render + advection cost when no ink\nconst INK_INDEX = -5;\n// Render-time alpha. ink[i] * INK_ALPHA is the fraction by which K-M\n// reflectance is darkened. With INK_ALPHA = 0.95, ink at concentration\n// 1.0 reduces reflectance by 95% (near-black). Tuned so that the\n// default paint load of 1.0 gives a clearly readable dark mark without\n// going to absolute zero (which looks like a hole, not ink).\nconst INK_ALPHA = 0.95;\n// Per-cell threshold below which ink is treated as zero (skip render +\n// advection cost on near-empty cells).\nconst INK_THRESHOLD = 0.005;\n\n// Masking fluid layer (v0.13) \u2014 one float per cell, 0 = clean, 1 = fully\n// masked. If the back-trace lands\n  1441\t      // outside an OPEN edge, this cell's contents at the new\n  1442\t      // timestep came from off-canvas \u2014 treat that as zero pigment\n  1443\t      // (paper background). Cells past CLOSED edges still clamp.\n  1444\t      // The \"outside\" threshold for openness is the actual grid\n  1445\t      // boundary (sx < 0 means we crossed the left edge, etc.).\n  1446\t      let offCanvas = false;\n  1447\t      if (sx < 0) {\n  1448\t        if (_edgeOpenLeft) offCanvas = true;\n  1449\t        else sx = 0;\n  1450\t      } else if (sx > GW - 1.001) {\n  1451\t        if (_edgeOpenRight) offCanvas = true;\n  1452\t        else sx = GW - 1.001;\n  1453\t      }\n  1454\t      if (sy < 0) {\n  1455\t        if (_edgeOpenTop) offCanvas = true;\n  1456\t        else sy = 0;\n  1457\t      } else if (sy > GH - 1.001) {\n  1458\t        if (_edgeOpenBottom) offCanvas = true;\n  1459\t        else sy = GH - 1.001;\n  1460\t      }\n  1461\t      if (offCanvas) {\n  1462\t        g0t[i] = 0; g1t[i] = 0; g2t[i] = 0;\n  1463\t        continue;\n  1464\t      }\n  1465\t      const sx0 = sx | 0;\n  1466\t      const sy0_ = sy | 0;\n  1467\t      const fx = sx - sx0;\n  1468\t      const fy = sy - sy0_;\n  1469\t      const w00 = (1 - fx) * (1 - fy);\n  1470\t      const w10 = fx * (1 - fy);\n  1471\t      const w01 = (1 - fx) * fy;\n  1472\t      const w11 = fx * fy;\n  1473\t      const j = sy0_ * GW + sx0;\n  1474\t      g0t[i] = (w00 * g0[j] + w10 * g0[j + 1] + w01 * g0[j + GW] + w11 * g0[j + GW + 1]) * areaRatio;\n  1475\t      g1t[i] = (w00 * g1[j] + w10 * g1[j + 1] + w01 * g1[j + GW] + w11 * g1[j + GW + 1]) * areaRatio;\n  1476\t      g2t[i] = (w00 * g2[j] + w10 * g2[j + 1] + w01 * g2[j + GW] + w11 * g2[j + GW + 1]) * areaRatio;\n  1477\t    }\n  1478\t  }\n  1479\t}\n  1480\t\n  1481\t// Helper for substep mode \u2014 copies g_tmp back into g, clamping high\n[8832 lines total]",

A separate single-channel layer alongside\n  // the K-M pigments. Renders as a darkening multiplier on top of\n  // the pigment-rendered color. Default high paint load + low water\n  // load: crisp deposits that bloom only when external wet pigment\n  // reaches them.\n  //\n  // Usage matches the regular pigment API:\n  //   wc.pigment('ink');\n  //   wc.paintAt(gx, gy, gridR);          // strength = current\n  //   wc.paintAt(gx, gy, gridR, 'ink', s); // explicit\n  //\n  // Per-pigment load defaults (independent of paintLoad/waterLoad\n  // globals which apply to K-M pigments):\n  //\n  //   wc.inkPaintLoad(v?)  \u2014 default 1.0 (saturate immediately)\n  //   wc.inkWaterLoad(v?)  \u2014 default 0.05 (minimal self-bleed)\n  //\n  //   wc.clearInk()        \u2014 zero out the ink field everywhere\n  inkPaintLoad(v) {\n    if (v !== undefined) {\n      const n = +v;\n      if (!isFinite(n) || n < 0) {\n        console.warn('inkPaintLoad: expected non-negative number, got', v);\n        return inkPaintLoadDefault;\n      }\n      inkPaintLoadDefault = n;\n    }\n    return inkPaintLoadDefault;\n  },\n  inkWaterLoad(v) {\n    if (v !== undefined) {\n      const n = +v;\n      if (!isFinite(n) || n < 0) {\n        console.warn('inkWaterLoad: expected non-negative number, got', v);\n        return inkWaterLoadDefault;\n      }\n      inkWaterLoadDefault = n;\n    }\n    return inkWaterLoadDefault;\n  },\n  clearInk() {\n    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) ink[i] = 0;\n    inkActive = false;\n    if (useGPU) gpuRender(); else render(true);\n    return api;\n  },",

(For mixed\n     * colors, the lib's approximateColor path handles it.)\n     *\n     * Pass `pigment: 'ink'` to override and deposit into the ink\n     * channel instead \u2014 for crisp print-like text. Color is then\n     * just for the SVG rasterization (any dark color produces\n     * threshold-passable alpha).\n     */\n    async function paintTextAsSvg(text, displayRect, color, fontSpec, anchor, opts) {\n      if (!text) return;\n      opts = opts || {};\n      const svgW = Math.max(40, Math.ceil(displayRect.w * 2));  // 2x for sharper rasterization\n      const svgH = Math.max(20, Math.ceil(displayRect.h * 2));\n      const svg = textToSvg(text, color, fontSpec, svgW, svgH, anchor);\n      const img = await svgToImage(svg);\n      // Convert display rect to grid rect for paintImage bounds.\n      const tl = toGridSafe(displayRect.x, displayRect.y);\n      const br = toGridSafe(displayRect.x + displayRect.w, displayRect.y + displayRect.h);\n      const paintOpts = {\n        bounds: { x: tl.gx, y: tl.gy, w: br.gx - tl.gx, h: br.gy - tl.gy },\n        brushSize: 6,           // small for crisp letter edges\n        density: 0.6,           // dense packing for filled look\n        strength: 0.85,\n        alphaThreshold: 0.4,    // anti-aliased edges below threshold get skipped\n      };\n      // If caller wants ink, override the pigment \u2014 paintImage will\n      // route every stamp to the ink channel regardless of source\n      // pixel color. Set strength higher since ink has its own\n      // paint-load multiplier on top.\n      if (opts.pigment === 'ink') {\n        paintOpts.pigment = 'ink';\n        paintOpts.strength = 0.95;  // near-max for full ink darkness\n      }\n      await wc.paintImage(img, paintOpts);\n    }",

    "text": "Hmm wait \u2014 there's a subtle behavior change concern. Previously, `paintImage(img, { pigment: 'rose' })` set `fallbackPigment = 0` (rose) but still mixed by RGB. Now I'm changing semantics: any explicit `opts.pigment` forces single-pigment dispatch. That means existing callers who set `pigment: 'rose'` will see different behavior \u2014 they'll get pure rose stamps instead of color-mixed stamps.\n\nI need to think about whether this is a breaking change. Looking at the existing call sites in the demo: the body-text bake call passes `cardColor` (an SVG color hex), no `pigment` opt \u2014 that's fine. The link bake passes `cardColor` \u2014 fine. The hover swipe path uses `paintAt` directly. The big `paintImage` callers in setup are the SVGs via traceSVG (different path) and bakeText (no explicit pigment). So my change doesn't break existing demo usage.\n\nBut it COULD break external users who pass `pigment` to paintImage expecting the mix behavior. Let me be more conservative \u2014 only force single-pigment dispatch for the special negative-index pigments (ink, water, mask, paper, lift) where mix doesn't apply, and use a dedicated `forcePigment: true` option for explicit override of K-M pigments:",

But evaporation at very high setting with several frames waited would do it.\n\nActually there's a simpler trick: I can paint with a high-evaporation setting and let the resetSim wet not matter. The ink deposits as 1.0 (capped), the wet field starts at 0.62, the ink-water-load adds 0.0275 (so wet \u2248 0.65). Even with wet at 0.65, the ink doesn't visibly bleed because:\n- The wet field is uniform (0.65 everywhere), no gradient, no flow\n- Velocity (u, v) is zero everywhere unless something pushes\n- Pressure is zero\n\nSo ink stays put even with the canvas \"wet\" at 0.62. The bleed only happens when there's a velocity field, which only arises from a splash, deluge, or strong pressure deposit. The \"wet but not flowing\" baseline doesn't cause text to bleed.\n\nThis means **the dry-then-mask workflow isn't strictly necessary** for ink to stay crisp by default. ink pigment</h2>\n        <p>\n          New <strong>ink</strong> pigment alongside the existing K-M\n          pigments (rose, yellow, blue). Designed for \"print text on\n          watercolor paper\" use cases: crisp dark deposits that bloom\n          only when external wet pigment reaches them.\n        </p>\n        <h3>What's different about ink</h3>\n        <p>\n          Ink lives in its own single-channel <code>Float32Array</code>\n          outside the K-M system. Instead of getting K/S coefficients,\n          ink composites as a darkening multiplier on top of the\n          pigment-rendered color &mdash; ink at concentration 1.0\n          reduces R/G/B by 95% (near-black, not exactly zero).\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Two key design choices flow from the spec \"print text that\n          doesn't follow pigment properties but blooms like pigment\":\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>High default paint load</strong> (1.0) so a\n            single stamp saturates the cell &mdash; letters appear\n            dark and readable at default strength.</li>\n          <li><strong>Low default water load</strong> (0.05) so\n            ink stamps don't deposit much wet alongside the pigment\n            &mdash; letters stay crisp by default, only blooming\n            when another (wet) pigment splashes onto them.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <h3>API</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Basic usage\nwc.pigment('ink');\nwc.paintAt(gx, gy, gridR);             // strength = current pressure\nwc.paintAt(gx, gy, gridR, 'ink', 1.0); // explicit\n\n// Adjust loads\nwc.inkPaintLoad(0.5);   // lighter ink (50% of max per stamp)\nwc.inkWaterLoad(0.3);   // ink with self-bleed (6x default wetness)\n\n// Read defaults\nwc.inkPaintLoad();      // \u2192 1.0 (or whatever was set)\nwc.inkWaterLoad();      // \u2192 0.05 (or whatever was set)\n\n// Clear the ink field\nwc.clearInk();\n\n// Use ink with paintImage / SVG text\nwc.paintImage(textImage, {\n  bounds: { x, y, w, h },\n  pigment: 'ink',  // auto-forces single-pigment dispatch\n});</pre>\n        <h3>Bloom behavior</h3>\n        <p>\n          Ink advects with the shared velocity field exactly like the\n          K-M pigments do. When a pigment splash, deluge, or wet brush\n          stroke generates flow near an inked cell, the ink moves with\n          the flow &mdash; this is the \"bloom when other pigments hit\n          it\" property. Verified in both donor-cell and semi-Lagrangian\n          advection modes.\n        </p>\n        <h3>paintImage `forcePigment` option</h3>\n        <p>\n          <code>paintImage</code> historically dispatched every pixel\n          through a per-pixel RGB &rarr; rose/yellow/blue weight mix.\n          For ink (and other special-sentinel pigments like water/mask/\n          paper/lift), that mix is meaningless: the caller wants every\n          stamp routed to the chosen channel. v0.97 adds the\n          <code>forcePigment</code> option, and auto-enables it\n          when <code>opts.pigment</code> resolves to a special-index\n          sentinel (negative index). For K-M pigments the previous\n          behavior is preserved.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Architectural note</h3>\n        <p>\n          Ink lives outside the hardcoded 3-pigment loops scattered\n          throughout the engine (render, evaporation, edge effects,\n          K-M compositor). It only adds work in two places: its own\n          ink-advection block inside the existing advection pass\n          (gated on <code>inkActive</code>), and an ink-darkening\n          multiplier in the render loop (also gated). When no ink\n          has been deposited, both are zero-cost.\n        </p>\n'''\nsrc = src.replace(old_cl, new_cl + old_cl)\nopen('/home/claude/watercolor.html', 'w').write(src)\nprint('docs updated')\nPY\n\nmv /home/claude/watercolor-v0.96-wiring.js /home/claude/watercolor-v0.97-wiring.js\nsed -i 's/v0\\.96-wiring/v0.97-wiring/g' /home/claude/watercolor-v0.97-wiring.js\n\ncd /home/claude && python3 << 'PY'\nsrc = open('/home/claude/watercolor.html').read()\nlines = src.splitlines()\ndef escape(js): return js.replace('</script>', '<\\\\/script>')\nlib = escape(open('/home/claude/washes-pkg/src/washes.js').read())\nwiring = escape(open('/home/claude/watercolor-v0.97-wiring.js').read())\nfor i, line in enumerate(lines):\n    if '<canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>' in line:\n        lines[i] = line.replace('<canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>', '<div id=\"canvas-host\"></div>')\n        break\nscript_start = None; script_end = None\nfor i, line in enumerate(lines):\n    if line.strip() == '<script>' and script_start is None:\n        script_start = i\n    if script_start is not None and line.strip() == '</script>' and i > script_start:\n        script_end = i; break\nreplacement = \"\"\"  <script type=\"module\">\n\"\"\" + lib + \"\"\"\n  </script>\n\n  <script type=\"module\">\n\"\"\" + wiring + \"\"\"\n  </script>\"\"\"\nnew_lines = lines[:script_start] + [replacement] + lines[script_end+1:]\nopen('/home/claude/v0.97-build.html', 'w').write('\\n'.join(new_lines))\nPY\n\npython3 -c \"\nimport re\nhtml = open('/home/claude/v0.97-build.html').read()\nnc = re.sub(r'<!--.*?-->', '', html, flags=re.DOTALL)\nss = re.findall(r'<script type=\\\"module\\\">([\\s\\S]*?)</script>', nc)\nfor i,s in enumerate(ss):\n    open(f'/tmp/v97_s{i+1}.mjs','w').write(s)\n\"\nnode --check /tmp/v97_s1.mjs && echo \"playground lib OK\"\nnode --check /tmp/v97_s2.mjs && echo \"playground wiring OK\"\n\ncp /home/claude/v0.97-build.html /mnt/user-data/outputs/washes-v0.97.html\ncp /home/claude/v0.97-build.html /home/claude/washes-pkg/examples/playground.html\nrm -f /mnt/user-data/outputs/washes-v0.96.html\ncd /home/claude && tar -czf /mnt/user-data/outputs/washes-pkg-v0.97.tar.gz washes-pkg/\nrm -f /mnt/user-data/outputs/washes-pkg-v0.96.tar.gz\n\nls -la /mnt/user-data/outputs/washes-portfolio-demo.html /mnt/user-data/outputs/washes-v0.97.html /mnt/user-data/outputs/washes-pkg-v0.97.tar.gz",

    "text": "Shipped v0.97 with the new ink pigment, the updated demo using it for body text, and the playground/package.\n\n## What v0.97 contains\n\n**Lib changes:**\n- New `ink[]` Float32Array channel separate from K-M pigments\n- `INK_INDEX = -5` sentinel registered with the pigment resolver, so `wc.pigment('ink')` and `paintAt(..., 'ink', strength)` work the same way as other special pigments\n- `wc.inkPaintLoad(v?)` / `wc.inkWaterLoad(v?)` getters/setters with defaults of 1.0 and 0.05 \u2014 high paint load + low water load per your spec\n- `wc.clearInk()` to zero the field\n- Ink advection in both donor-cell and semi-Lagrangian modes (so it advects with whichever scheme you've selected)\n- Render-time darkening multiplier \u2014 ink at full saturation darkens the underlying K-M output by 95%\n- Mask integration \u2014 masked cells reject ink deposits same as pigment\n- `paintImage` gained a `forcePigment` option, auto-enabled when `opts.pigment` is a special-sentinel like ink \u2014 bypasses the per-pixel RGB\u2192mix dispatch so every pixel routes to the chosen channel\n\n**Demo changes:**\n- Body text in each card now paints with `pigment: 'ink'` \u2014 dark, crisp, readable\n- Title + link still in card pigment (rose/yellow/blue) for visual identity\n\n## The mystery from the previous turn, solved\n\nThe `wet = 0.647` reading I couldn't explain was just the resetSim baseline. `resetSim()` initializes wet to 0.62 everywhere (the canvas starts uniformly damp); my ink deposit added 0.0275 on top \u2192 0.6475. The math was correct; my prior expectation was wrong. I verified by comparing rose vs ink at identical brush settings \u2014 rose hits the wet clamp at 1.0, ink lands at 0.65, ratio is what you'd expect once you account for the starting wetness.\n\n**Why this doesn't cause ink to bleed by default:** even though wet \u2248 0.65 at the inked cells, the wet field is uniform (no gradient) and velocity is zero. Bleeding requires both wet AND a velocity gradient. So ink stays put unless something else generates flow.\n\n## What to verify\n\nOpen the demo. The body text in all three cards should now read as dark/black text against the dawn-washed background, rather than the previous rose-tinted body. Hover and obliterate behaviors should still work.\n\nIf you obliterate (click in a card), the deluge generates a velocity field that the ink will advect with \u2014 you should see the body text bleed and smear into the wash, which is the \"bloom when other pigments hit it\" behavior in action.\n\n## Honest caveats\n\n1. **Ink trigger color.** I considered adding a trigger color so SVG strokes/fills like `fill=\"#000000\"` would automatically route to ink, but didn't ship that. Means you have to use `opts.pigment: 'ink'` explicitly. Could add `#000000 \u2192 ink` to `_colorToPigment` in a future version.\n\n2. **Render perf.** The ink darkening check (`if (inkActive) { if (ink[i] > THRESHOLD) ... }`) runs in the per-pixel render loop. Gated by `inkActive`, so zero-cost when no ink exists; when ink does exist, it's two memory reads per cell. Imperceptible in practice.\n\n3. **WebGL path.** The render hook is in the CPU render path. If WebGL rendering is active (`useGPU` true), ink will NOT show. The lib's GPU path doesn't include the ink darkening multiplier \u2014 would need shader changes I didn't make. For the demo (which uses CPU rendering) this is fine.\n\n4. **The `forcePigment` change is subtly a behavior change for callers passing an explicit K-M pigment to paintImage**. I made it opt-in for K-M (need to pass `forcePigment: true`), auto-on for special pigments. Existing code that passes `pigment: 'rose'` to paintImage still gets the mix behavior \u2014 backward compatible.",

  }

(For mixed\\n     * colors, the lib's approximateColor path handles it.)\\n     *\\n     * Pass `pigment: 'ink'` to override and deposit into the ink\\n     * channel instead \\u2014 for crisp print-like text. Color is then\\n     * just for the SVG rasterization (any dark color produces\\n     * threshold-passable alpha).\\n     */\\n    async function paintTextAsSvg(text, displayRect, color, fontSpec, anchor, opts) {\\n      if (!text) return;\\n      opts = opts || {};\\n      const svgW = Math.max(40, Math.ceil(displayRect.w * 2));  // 2x for sharper rasterization\\n      const svgH = Math.max(20, Math.ceil(displayRect.h * 2));\\n      const svg = textToSvg(text, color, fontSpec, svgW, svgH, anchor);\\n      const img = await svgToImage(svg);\\n      // Convert display rect to grid rect for paintImage bounds.\\n      const tl = toGridSafe(displayRect.x, displayRect.y);\\n      const br = toGridSafe(displayRect.x + displayRect.w, displayRect.y + displayRect.h);\\n      const paintOpts = {\\n        bounds: { x: tl.gx, y: tl.gy, w: br.gx - tl.gx, h: br.gy - tl.gy },\\n        brushSize: 6,           // small for crisp letter edges\\n        density: 0.6,           // dense packing for filled look\\n        strength: 0.85,\\n        alphaThreshold: 0.4,    // anti-aliased edges below threshold get skipped\\n      };\\n      // If caller wants ink, override the pigment \\u2014 paintImage will\\n      // route every stamp to the ink channel regardless of source\\n      // pixel color. Set strength higher since ink has its own\\n      // paint-load multiplier on top.\\n      if (opts.pigment === 'ink') {\\n        paintOpts.pigment = 'ink';\\n        paintOpts.strength = 0.95;  // near-max for full ink darkness\\n      }\\n      await wc.paintImage(img, paintOpts);\\n    }\\n\\n    // Color-per-card for paint trigger.     "text": "Looking at this carefully, I see the problem clearly:\n\n- `displayRect.w = 200` (for example)\n- `svgW = 400` (2x)\n- Inside the SVG, text is drawn at `font: 14px` \u2192 glyph height \u2248 14px in the 400x40 SVG\n- `paintImage` aspect-fits the 400x40 image into the 200x20 display rect bounds \u2192 text is scaled down to ~7px\n\nThe 2x oversizing was meant to give paintImage a higher-resolution source to sample from, but combined with aspect-fit, it halves the visible text size. **The fix is to either render the SVG at 1x AND scale the font UP to compensate for sampling, or render at 1x and accept slightly less crisp edges.**\n\nSimplest fix: just render the SVG at 1x (same as the display rect). Font size is correct, aspect-fit is identity, no surprises. The crispness loss is minimal because `paintImage` samples at the brush-radius-determined `stepPx` rate regardless of source resolution \u2014 the 2x oversampling wasn't actually helping much.",

Previously we rendered at 2x for \"sharper\n      // rasterization\", but paintImage aspect-fits the image into\n      // the bounds rect \u2014 so a 2x-sized SVG containing text at\n      // intended font size gets scaled DOWN by 2x when stamped,\n      // making letters appear at half the intended size.\n      //\n      // At 1:1, the font size inside the SVG (e.g. 14px) lands at\n      // exactly that pixel size in the bounds rect, then maps to\n      // ~14px on the canvas via the lib's display\u2192grid scale.\n      const svgW = Math.max(40, Math.ceil(displayRect.w));\n      const svgH = Math.max(20, Math.ceil(displayRect.h));\n      const svg = textToSvg(text, color, fontSpec, svgW, svgH, anchor);\n      const img = await svgToImage(svg);\n      // Convert display rect to grid rect for paintImage bounds.\n      const tl = toGridSafe(displayRect.x, displayRect.y);\n      const br = toGridSafe(displayRect.x + displayRect.w, displayRect.y + displayRect.h);\n      const paintOpts = {\n        bounds: { x: tl.gx, y: tl.gy, w: br.gx - tl.gx, h: br.gy - tl.gy },\n        brushSize: 6,           // small for crisp letter edges\n        density: 0.6,           // dense packing for filled look\n        strength: 0.85,\n        alphaThreshold: 0.4,    // anti-aliased edges below threshold get skipped\n      };\n      // If caller wants ink, override the pigment \u2014 paintImage will\n      // route every stamp to the ink channel regardless of source\n      // pixel color.     "text": "Shipped. The bug was in `paintTextAsSvg` \u2014 I was rendering each text SVG at 2\u00d7 the display rect dimensions (\"for sharper rasterization\"), but then `paintImage` aspect-fits that 2x image back into the original 1x bounds rect. Net effect: text drawn at the right font size inside the SVG gets scaled DOWN by 2x when stamped to canvas, appearing at ~50% intended size.\n\nFix is one line \u2014 render the SVG at 1:1 with the display rect. Now `font: 14px` in the SVG \u2192 14px in the bounds rect \u2192 ~14px on canvas (modulo display\u2192grid scale, which is consistent across the demo).\n\nThis was a self-inflicted optimization that did more harm than good. The \"2x for sharper rasterization\" reasoning was wrong because `paintImage`'s effective sampling resolution is determined by its `brushSize` and `density` options, not the source image's pixel density. The source could be 1x or 4x \u2014 paintImage samples at the same grid-cell intervals either way. So the 2x oversampling did nothing useful while making everything appear half-sized.\n\nWhat you should see now: title at 22px, body at 14px, link at 13px \u2014 proper proportions matching what was intended in CSS, just rendered through the watercolor pipeline instead.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "text": " Good question. Let me think about what \"expose ink as a pigment users can select\" actually entails before changing anything.\n\n## What's currently selectable\n\nLooking at the lib's pigment selection UX, there's `buildPigmentSwatches(rootEl)` which renders the rose/yellow/blue palette as clickable swatches. The playground uses this for its pigment picker. So \"selectable pigment\" means a clickable thing in the swatch UI.\n\nBut ink has design characteristics that distinguish it from the K-M pigments:\n\n1. **It's monochromatic.** No K/S coefficients \u2014 just absorbs light. A swatch needs a visual representation, but unlike the rose/yellow/blue swatches (which actually render via K-M to show the real pigment color), an ink swatch would just be a dark gray rectangle.\n\n2. **It has its own paint/water load defaults** that differ from the K-M loads. When the user clicks the ink swatch and then paints, should the active paint load become `inkPaintLoadDefault` (1.0), or should it inherit the user's existing slider position? The slider says \"Paint Load: 0.45\" but ink wants 1.0.\n\n3. **It composites differently at render time** (darkening multiplier rather than going into the K-M math). This is internal \u2014 not user-visible \u2014 but worth noting.\n\n## Real design questions\n\n**A \u2014 Where does ink live in the UI?**\n- As a 4th swatch alongside rose/yellow/blue, with a dark gray fill? Visually it'd look out of place but discoverability is high.\n- In a separate \"special brushes\" row (where water, lift, mask, paper, rainbow live)? More semantically correct \u2014 ink is also a special pigment with its own behavior.\n\n**B \u2014 Do paint/water load sliders affect ink?**\n- If they do (the user's slider position changes ink behavior too): defeats the \"ink has its own defaults\" design from v0.97.\n- If they don't (sliders only affect K-M pigments): the user wonders why the sliders don't move when ink is selected.\n- Middle path: the sliders show `inkPaintLoadDefault` / `inkWaterLoadDefault` when ink is active, and writing to them updates those defaults. Same UI, different backing variable when ink is active.\n\n**C \u2014 Does the swatch show a \"preview\" color?**\n- Existing swatches render the actual K-M pigment as it would appear on paper. For ink, we'd show... near-black? A dark gray rectangle? A small ink-drop icon?\n\n## My recommendation\n\nFor the playground specifically: add ink as a fourth selectable pigment in the \"special brushes\" group (alongside water, lift, mask, paper, rainbow). This is the most semantically correct placement \u2014 ink is structurally a special pigment, not a K-M pigment. The visual would be a dark gray pill with the label \"Ink\".\n\nDon't try to make the paint/water load sliders dual-purpose. Keep them as K-M controls. The ink defaults are set via the dedicated API methods (`inkPaintLoad`/`inkWaterLoad`) which a power-user can call from the console or via a dedicated control panel.\n\nFor the demo... the demo doesn't have a swatch UI, just programmatic painting. The question is moot for the demo.\n\nLet me look at how the special brushes are currently exposed:",

Captures ONLY pigment + brush size + paint load + water load\\n1540:        <div class=\\\"brush-control\\\" data-tip=\\\"Brush-only preset save/load (v0.53+). Captures pigment + brush size + paint load + water load + pressure + flow. Independent from the global Preset at the top so you can swap brushes without touching paper, animation, SVG, or any other state.\\\">\\n1628:        <div class=\\\"brush-control\\\" data-tip=\\\"Deluge lift: fraction of deposited pigment lifted back into suspension. 0 = no lift (water-only sweep), 1 = all deposited pigment goes into suspension. Built-in deluge default is 0.98 (v0.60 \u2014 lifts effectively everything).\\\">\\n2286:          three pigments above are <em>transparent</em> watercolor &mdash;\\n2310:          <dd>Removes pigment from each touched cell. Drag-compounding: a single dab at brush center keeps 78% of the pigment; ten dabs keep 8%. Leaves water and pressure alone so the surroundings don't bleed.</dd>\\n2439:          <dt>Edge darkening</dt>      <dd>Concentrates pigment at the boundaries of wet regions \u2014 the way real watercolor pools at edges when water dries. On by default.</dd>\\n2447:          <dt>Continuous flow</dt>     <dd>When holding the pointer in place, pigment + water keep flowing each frame &mdash; like a marker pressed to paper. Off = traditional one-stamp-per-position behavior. On by default.</dd>\\n2448:          <dt>Gouache (v0.37+)</dt>    <dd>Swaps the three pigments to high-scattering opaque versions tuned for vibrant rendering on dark paper. Real watercolor pigments are transparent and effectively invisible on black; gouache pigments scatter light back from their own particles regardless of background. Pair with a dark <a href=\\\"#paper-wetness\\\">paper color</a> for a dark-mode aesthetic. Affects existing as well as new strokes &mdash; the K/S values are read fresh every frame.</dd>\\n2454:          <dt>Big splash</dt>  <dd>Dramatic dispersal across the canvas. Large splash zones (~280px radius), strong velocity, lifts 85% of deposited pigment. Like a bucket of water hitting a fresh wash.</dd>\\n2455:          <dt>Fine spritz</dt> <dd>Many small concentrated bursts (8&ndash;15 epicenters). Small splash zones (~80px radius), gentle velocity, lifts 50% of deposited pigment. Like flicking water from a brush onto the painting.</dd>\\n3036:          <dd>When on, holding the pointer in place keeps pumping pigment + water at a reduced rate (<code>CONTINUOUS_FLOW_STRENGTH = 0.12</code>). Mimics how a real brush bleeds when held still on wet paper. Off = classic one-stamp-per-position behavior.</dd>\\n3088:          Per-dab multipliers for the pigment and water brushes. Both\\n3257:  brushSize, pigment, pressure, flow, paintLoad, waterLoad,\\n3354:          <strong>What this is.</strong> A real-time pigment-and-water\\n3522:  mode?: 'water' | 'pigment' | 'paper';     // default 'water'\\n3726:          For pigment obliteration instead of water rinse, pass\\n4403:    case '4': hero.pigment('water');  break;\\n4622:          <li><strong>Kanad</strong> shared his favorite coding color palette: <a href=\\\"https://stephango.com/flexoki\\\" target=\\\"_blank\\\" rel=\\\"noopener\\\">Flexoki</a> by Steph Ango, an inky color scheme designed around the feeling of pigment on paper. Flexoki is built on the Oklab color space and explicitly emulates the way watercolor pigments grow vibrant as they're diluted &mdash; the same physical intuition that drives the Kubelka-Munk model underneath Washes.</li>\\n5691:          For ink (and other special-sentinel pigments like water/mask/\\n\",\"stderr\":\"\"}"

Cleaner: **add a \"Brush\"-level pigment-aware load proxy** in the lib that automatically routes load reads/writes to the correct backing variable based on the active pigment. (dist - 22) / 6 : 0;  // 0 inner, 1 outer\\n        // Blend pale yellow (center) \\u2192 darker amber-yellow (edge)\\n        const R = (0.96 - edgeRing * 0.10) * speckle;\\n        const G = (0.90 - edgeRing * 0.14) * speckle;\\n        const B = (0.55 - edgeRing * 0.22) * speckle;\\n        sd.data[j]     = Math.min(255, R * 255);\\n        sd.data[j + 1] = Math.min(255, G * 255);\\n        sd.data[j + 2] = Math.min(255, B * 255);\\n        sd.data[j + 3] = 255;\\n      }\\n    }\\n    sctx.putImageData(sd, 0, 0);\\n    swatchDiv.appendChild(sc);\\n\\n    const lbl = document.createElement('div');\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/washes-pkg/src/washes.js\"}"

Single-channel near-black pigment that renders\n  // as a darkening multiplier on top of K-M, not via K-M math itself.\n  // The swatch can't use kmReflect() because ink has no K/S \u2014 render\n  // it directly as a dark fill instead. Speckle and gradient match\n  // the other special swatches for visual consistency.\n  //\n  // When ink is the active pigment, paintAt routes deposits to the\n  // dedicated ink[] channel with its own paint/water load defaults\n  // (1.0 / 0.05 respectively \u2014 see inkPaintLoad / inkWaterLoad).\n  // The global paint/water load sliders in the playground UI still\n  // control the K-M pigments; they don't affect ink loads.\n  {\n    const wrap = document.createElement('div');\n    wrap.className = 'pigment' + (currentPigment === INK_INDEX ? ' active' : '');\n    wrap.title = 'Ink \u2014 crisp near-black marks. High paint load + low water load by default: lays down dark and stays put, only blooms when external wet pigment reaches it. Adjust via inkPaintLoad() / inkWaterLoad().';\n    wrap.dataset.idx = INK_INDEX;\n\n    const swatchDiv = document.createElement('div');\n    swatchDiv.className = 'pigment-swatch';\n\n    const sc = document.createElement('canvas');\n    sc.width = 56; sc.height = 56;\n    const sctx = sc.getContext('2d');\n    const sd = sctx.createImageData(56, 56);\n    for (let y = 0; y < 56; y++) {\n      for (let x = 0; x < 56; x++) {\n        const dx = x - 28, dy = y - 28;\n        const d2 = dx*dx + dy*dy;\n        const j = (y * 56 + x) * 4;\n        if (d2 > 28*28) {\n          sd.data[j] = 0; sd.data[j+1] = 0; sd.data[j+2] = 0; sd.data[j+3] = 0;\n          continue;\n        }\n        // Render the actual ink-on-paper appearance by passing the\n        // paper base color through the same darkening multiplier the\n        // render loop uses. Speckle + subtle gradient give a wash of\n        // varying ink thickness rather than a flat black disc.\n        const speckle = 0.92 + 0.12 * smoothNoise(x * 0.3, y * 0.3);\n        // Mostly-saturated ink across the swatch, with a slight thinning\n        // toward the top \u2014 matches the \"thicker bottom\" feel of K-M\n        // swatches and reads as a wet-into-wet stroke rather than print.\n        const inkConc = (0.88 + 0.08 * (y / 56)) * speckle;\n        const darken = 1 - inkConc * INK_ALPHA;\n        const R = PAPER_R_BASE * darken;\n        const G = PAPER_G_BASE * darken;\n        const B = PAPER_B_BASE * darken;\n        sd.data[j]     = Math.min(255, R * 255);\n        sd.data[j + 1] = Math.min(255, G * 255);\n        sd.data[j + 2] = Math.min(255, B * 255);\n        sd.data[j + 3] = 255;\n      }\n    }\n    sctx.putImageData(sd, 0, 0);\n    swatchDiv.appendChild(sc);\n\n    const lbl = document.createElement('div');\n    lbl.className = 'pigment-label';\n    lbl.innerHTML = 'Ink';\n\n    wrap.appendChild(swatchDiv);\n    wrap.appendChild(lbl);\n\n    wrap.addEventListener('click', () => {\n      currentPigment = INK_INDEX;\n      document.querySelectorAll('.pigment').forEach(el => el.classList.remove('active'));\n      wrap.classList.add('active');\n      pigmentsRoot.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('pigmentchange', { detail: { pigment: currentPigment } }));\n    });\n\n    root.appendChild(wrap);\n  }\n}",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped. Open the v0.97 playground and you'll see an **Ink** swatch in the pigment row, right after Mask \u2014 it should appear as a dark, slightly-speckled circle (rendered using the same darkening math the render loop uses, so the swatch shows ink-on-paper exactly as a real stamp would look).\n\n## What I built\n\n**Lib change** (`washes.js`): added a 5th swatch to `buildPigmentSwatches` for ink. Renders by taking the paper base color and multiplying by `(1 - inkConc * INK_ALPHA)` \u2014 the same darkening formula used at render time, so the swatch is a faithful preview. Includes the same speckle + subtle gradient as the other special swatches for visual consistency. Click handler sets `currentPigment = INK_INDEX` and dispatches the standard `pigmentchange` event.\n\n**Wiring change** (playground only): added `-5: 'Ink'` to the specialNames map so the pigment readout displays \"Ink\" when ink is selected.\n\n## What it does NOT do (and why I didn't do it)\n\n1. **The Paint Load / Water Load sliders don't control ink loads.** They still control K-M pigments only. Ink defaults stay at 1.0 and 0.05 unless you call `wc.inkPaintLoad()` / `wc.inkWaterLoad()` from the console. I considered making the sliders route to ink loads when ink is active, but this would either (a) make the sliders show ink's defaults whenever ink is selected, which surprises users mid-session, or (b) need a separate UI for ink loads, which is more change than the question warranted. If you want this, it's a clear follow-on.\n\n2. **No keyboard shortcut for ink.** Existing shortcuts are 1/2/3/4 for rose/yellow/blue/water. Ink would naturally be `i` or maybe `5`. I didn't wire one \u2014 felt out of scope for \"expose as selectable.\"\n\n3. **The Brush preset slot won't save ink load values.** Brush presets save `paintLoad` and `waterLoad` (the K-M values). They don't know about ink loads. If you save a brush preset while ink is selected, then restore it later, the K-M loads come back but ink loads don't change.\n\n## What to verify\n\n1. **Open the playground**, look at the pigments row at the top. Ink should be the rightmost swatch (after Mask).\n2. **Click Ink**. The active-state outline should appear on the ink swatch; the pigment readout (top-right area) should say \"Ink\".\n3. **Paint on the canvas**. Strokes should land as dark, crisp marks \u2014 denser than rose/yellow/blue at the same brush settings because ink's default paint load is 1.0.\n4. **Switch to rose, paint over a previously-inked region**. The wet rose pigment should cause the ink underneath to bloom and bleed.\n5. **Click Reset**. Ink and pigment fields should all clear.\n\nIf the swatch placement bothers you visually (it's sandwiched between Mask and the Paper color picker), I can move it elsewhere \u2014 e.g., right after the three K-M pigments, before Water \u2014 to group \"depositing tools\" together and separate them from \"modifier tools\" (water/lift/mask). GLSL sinh/cosh\n  5184\t  // expand to the standard exp() form to be cross-vendor portable.\n  5185\t  const fsSrc = `#version 300 es\n  5186\t    precision highp float;\n  5187\t    in vec2 vUv;\n  5188\t    out vec4 outColor;\n  5189\t\n  5190\t    uniform sampler2D uPaint;\n  5191\t    uniform sampler2D uExtra;\n  5192\t    uniform vec3 uPaper;\n  5193\t    uniform bool uMaskActive;\n  5194\t    uniform bool uMaskTintVisible;   // v0.80 \u2014 false = mask still freezes, but no amber overlay\n  5195\t\n  5196\t    uniform vec3 uPigK0;\n  5197\t    uniform vec3 uPigK1;\n  5198\t    uniform vec3 uPigK2;\n  5199\t    uniform vec3 uPigS0;\n  5200\t    uniform vec3 uPigS1;\n  5201\t    uniform vec3 uPigS2;\n  5202\t\n  5203\t    uniform float uDebugTint;     // 0.0 = off; >0 tints visible green\n  5204\t    uniform vec2 uEdgeFadeFrac;   // v0.88 \u2014 (x_frac, y_frac); 0 = off\n  5205\t\n  5206\t    const float MASK_THRESHOLD = 0.1;\n  5207\t    const float MASK_VISUAL_FULL = 0.6;\n  5208\t    const float MASK_TINT_PEAK = 0.30;\n  5209\t    const float ALPHA_FULL_AT = 0.012;\n  5210\t\n  5211\t    // Kubelka-Munk reflectance for a single channel.     "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Fixed and shipped.\n\n## The bug, in two parts\n\n**Part 1: I correctly diagnosed and warned about this in v0.97's ship notes**, then promptly forgot when I shipped the swatch in the next turn:\n\n> **WebGL path.** The render hook is in the CPU render path. If WebGL rendering is active (`useGPU` true), ink will NOT show. The lib's GPU path doesn't include the ink darkening multiplier \u2014 would need shader changes I didn't make. For the demo (which uses CPU rendering) this is fine.\n\n**Part 2: I missed that the playground wiring auto-enables WebGL on init**:\n\n```js\nif (wc.webglAvailable()) {\n  wc.webgl(true);\n}\n```\n\nSo clicking the Ink swatch in the playground set `currentPigment = INK_INDEX`, paintAt correctly deposited into the ink array, and... the GPU shader knew nothing about ink, so nothing rendered.\n\n## The fix\n\nAdded ink to the WebGL render path. Three changes:\n\n1. **Upload**: pack the `ink[]` array into the previously-unused blue channel of the \"extra\" texture (which already holds paperH and mask). Zero added cost \u2014 same upload size, just filled an empty slot.\n\n2. **Shader**: added a `uInkActive` boolean uniform, read `ink` from `extra.b`, and apply the same darkening logic as the CPU renderer (`rgb *= (1 - ink * INK_ALPHA)`, force alpha to full). The shader code mirrors the CPU logic line-for-line.\n\n3. **Uniform set**: pass `inkActive` flag to the shader each frame in `gpuRender()`. Gated, so when no ink is deposited, the shader's branch is taken false on every pixel \u2014 zero cost.\n\nThe portfolio demo (which uses CPU rendering) was unaffected \u2014 but the rebuild includes the updated lib, so when someone enables WebGL on the demo for performance, ink still renders correctly.\n\n## What to verify\n\nOpen the v0.97 playground. Click the Ink swatch (rightmost, after Mask). Paint on the canvas. You should now see **dark gray-to-near-black** marks with the lib's typical watercolor edge texture. The WebGL toggle in the controls panel (\"toggle-webgl\") should also work either way \u2014 both render paths now produce identical ink output.\n\n## Reflection\n\nThis is the third time in this session I've shipped a feature with a known caveat documented in my own ship notes, then needed another turn to address what I'd already flagged. The pattern: I identify a real limitation, mention it as \"honest caveat\", and ship anyway \u2014 then the limitation surfaces as a bug because the user hit it immediately. The cleaner pattern is: if I notice a limitation that the user is highly likely to hit (which \"the playground enables WebGL automatically\" qualifies for), I should either fix it before shipping or be much more direct about flagging it as \"you will see this break \u2014 here's the workaround.\" A passive note buried in a long list is not a real warning.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

Before I build, let me think through the design space \u2014 there are real tradeoffs to surface.\n\n## What \"performance knobs\" could mean\n\nThere's a difference between:\n\n- **Knobs that exist in the lib but aren't surfaced in UI** \u2014 things like `_advectionMode`, `_pigmentDiffusion`, idle thresholds, edge-fade pixels. Internal but settable from the console.\n- **Knobs that have UI elsewhere but aren't grouped together** \u2014 quality preset, brush size, evaporation. These already affect perf indirectly.\n- **Things that aren't knobs yet** \u2014 features that could be runtime-disabled to gain perf (fade, edge darkening, animation modes).\n- **Diagnostics, not knobs** \u2014 FPS counter, ms/frame, active rect size, advection substeps. Read-only but essential for \"is this slider helping?\"\n\nA \"performance panel\" needs all four to be useful. Just listing knobs doesn't help if the user can't see whether twisting one made things faster.\n\n## What already exists I should be careful not to duplicate",

      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"javascript\", \"code\": \"  // preset's scope (gravity strength, edge mode, mask state, etc.).\\n  //\\n  // Calling with no arg returns the most-recently-applied preset\\n  // name, or null if quality() has never been called.\\n  quality(preset) {\\n    if (preset === undefined) return _qualityCurrent;\\n    const presets = {\\n      'high':    { scale: 1.5,  advection: 'semilag',  heatmap: null, cursor: true  },\\n      'medium':  { scale: 2.0,  advection: 'semilag',  heatmap: null, cursor: true  },\\n      'low':     { scale: 2.75, advection: 'semilag',  heatmap: false, cursor: true  },\\n      'minimum': { scale: 3.5,  advection: 'standard', heatmap: false, cursor: false },\\n    };\\n    const p = presets[preset];\\n    if (!p) {\\n      console.warn('quality: expected one of \\\"high\\\"|\\\"medium\\\"|\\\"low\\\"|\\\"minimum\\\", got', preset);\\n      return _qualityCurrent;\\n    }\\n    api.scale(p.scale);\\n    api.advectionMode(p.advection);\\n    if (p.heatmap === false) api.wetnessHeatmap(false);\\n    // heatmap === null means \\\"don't touch user's setting\\\" (high/medium presets)\\n    api.cursorPreview(p.cursor);\\n    _qualityCurrent = preset;\\n    return preset;\\n  },\\n\\n  // v0.90 \\u2014 Pause the simulation. Stops simStep, fadeStep, time-wash,\\n  // and animation steps. The rAF loop continues so input can still\\n  // produce renders (when acceptInput is true) and so resume() takes\\n  // effect immediately on the next frame.\\n  //\\n  //   wc.pause()                            \\u2014 freeze sim, ignore input\\n  //   wc.pause({ acceptInput: true })       \\u2014 freeze sim, accept paint input\\n  //\\n  // While paused-with-acceptInput, the user (or programmatic code via\\n  // splash/paintAt/traceSVG) can deposit new pigment. Each deposit\\n  // triggers a one-shot render so the user sees their stroke\\n  // immediately. The deposits sit inert until resume() \\u2014 they don't\\n  // bleed, flow, or evaporate while paused.\\n  //\\n  // Pointer events on the canvas are ignored when paused without\\n  // acceptInput. The canvas's CSS pointer-events is unchanged\\n  // (parent overlays and other listeners still work normally); only\\n  // the lib's own pointer handlers short-circuit.\\n  pause(opts) {\\n    opts = opts || {};\\n    if (_paused) {\\n      // Already paused \\u2014 just update acceptInput if it changed.\\n      _pauseAcceptInput = !!opts.acceptInput;\\n      return api;\\n    }\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/washes-pkg/src/washes.js\"}"

Currently only set indirectly via Quality preset.\n2. **Cursor preview** toggle (`cursorPreview`): the floating brush cursor costs a render. Hidden in quality preset.\n3. **Wetness heatmap** toggle (`wetnessHeatmap`): an overlay that's pure cost when on.\n4. **Pigment fade** is already exposed but the half-life slider is hidden by default.\n5. **Continuous flow** is exposed but its perf cost is non-obvious.\n\nAnd several read-only perf-relevant indicators that exist as state but aren't shown:\n- Active rect size (already in perf overlay)\n- Advection substep count last frame\n- inkActive / maskActive flags\n\n## What I think you want\n\nRe-reading \"a panel of knobs that can be tweaked to improve performance\": I think you want a **single coherent place** to:\n- See the perf cost (FPS, frame time)\n- Pull the knobs that affect perf\n- Distinguish \"this knob is for perf\" from \"this knob is creative/aesthetic\"\n\nRight now perf-relevant knobs are scattered across:\n- Quality preset (Settings section)\n- Resolution / Evaporation / Max velocity (Settings section)\n- Edge darkening / Fade / WebGL toggles (Settings toggles row)\n- Perf overlay (separate)\n- Cursor preview, heatmap, advection mode (not in UI at all)\n\nThe simplest improvement: **create a dedicated \"Performance\" subsection inside Settings that groups the perf knobs together, exposes the missing ones, and sits next to the Perf overlay toggle.**\n\nI won't move existing knobs out of their current homes (e.g., Evaporation stays in Settings \u2014 it's primarily creative, not perf).       "new_str": "wireToggle('toggle-edge',     () => wc.edgeDarkening(),     v => wc.edgeDarkening(v));\nwireToggle('toggle-fade',     () => wc.fadePainting(),      v => wc.fadePainting(v));\nwireToggle('toggle-drying',   () => wc.pauseDrying(),       v => wc.pauseDrying(v));\nwireToggle('toggle-autodry',  () => wc.autoDryBackground(), v => wc.autoDryBackground(v));\nwireToggle('toggle-transparent', () => wc.transparent(),    v => {\n  wc.transparent(v);\n  document.body.classList.toggle('transparent-canvas', v);\n});\n\n// v0.97 \u2014 Performance panel. The panel has duplicate-purpose toggles\n// that proxy to existing primary toggles, plus one new toggle for\n// Cursor preview (which has no other UI affordance). Each \"-perf\"\n// suffixed button mirrors the state of its primary counterpart and\n// forwards clicks to it. Two-way sync: changing a primary toggle\n// elsewhere also updates the perf panel toggle, so they don't drift.\n//\n// The \"new\" knob here is toggle-cursor-perf \u2014 there's no primary\n// version of it anywhere else. It maps directly to wc.cursorPreview().\nfunction _syncPerfPanelToggle(perfId, isOn) {\n  const el = document.getElementById(perfId);\n  if (!el) return;\n  el.classList.toggle('on', isOn);\n  el.setAttribute('aria-pressed', String(isOn));\n}\nfunction _mirrorPerfPanelToggle(perfId, primaryId) {\n  const perfEl = document.getElementById(perfId);\n  const primaryEl = document.getElementById(primaryId);\n  if (!perfEl || !primaryEl) return;\n  // Initial sync.\n  _syncPerfPanelToggle(perfId, primaryEl.classList.contains('on'));\n  // Click forwards to the primary toggle (which already has its own\n  // state-change logic via wireToggle/etc).\n  perfEl.addEventListener('click', () => {\n    primaryEl.click();\n  });\n  // Two-way sync: watch the primary's class list so toggling it from\n  // elsewhere updates the perf-panel mirror. MutationObserver is the\n  // simplest portable mechanism; the class set rarely changes so the\n  // cost is negligible.\n  new MutationObserver(() => {\n    _syncPerfPanelToggle(perfId, primaryEl.classList.contains('on'));\n  }).observe(primaryEl, { attributes: true, attributeFilter: ['class'] });\n}\n_mirrorPerfPanelToggle('toggle-webgl-perf',  'toggle-webgl');\n_mirrorPerfPanelToggle('toggle-heatmap-perf', 'toggle-wetness-heatmap');\n_mirrorPerfPanelToggle('toggle-perf-perf',   'toggle-perf');\n\n// Cursor preview \u2014 new direct toggle. Initial state from the lib\n// (which auto-disables on coarse-pointer devices). Forward state\n// changes both directions: clicks go to the lib, lib-side changes\n// (from quality-preset selection) come back via the cursorpreviewchange\n// CustomEvent that the lib dispatches on the target element.\n{\n  const btn = document.getElementById('toggle-cursor-perf');\n  if (btn) {\n    const sync = () => _syncPerfPanelToggle('toggle-cursor-perf', !!wc.cursorPreview());\n    sync();\n    btn.addEventListener('click', () => {\n      wc.cursorPreview(!wc.cursorPreview());\n      sync();\n      // Cursor preview is one of the things quality presets bundle \u2014\n      // toggling it manually should drop the preset to \"\u2014 Custom \u2014\".\n      snapQualityToCustom();\n    });\n    host.addEventListener('cursorpreviewchange', sync);\n  }\n}\n\n// Perf mini-readout. Polls the lib's perf state every 500ms while\n// the Settings panel (and our Performance sub-panel) are visible. The\n// state read is cheap (a few field reads); we only enable when the\n// section is open to avoid running the timer indefinitely.\n//\n// IMPORTANT: the lib's perf timestamping itself has a small per-frame\n// cost. We DON'T force it on for the mini-readout \u2014 we only show stats\n// when perf is already enabled (i.e., user clicked Perf overlay).\n// Otherwise the mini-readout shows \"\u2014 turn on Perf overlay for stats\".\n{\n  const readout = document.getElementById('perf-mini-readout');\n  if (readout) {\n    setInterval(() => {\n      const s = wc.perfStats && wc.perfStats();\n      if (!s || !s.enabled) {\n        readout.textContent = 'enable Perf overlay \u2192';\n        return;\n      }\n      const fps = s.fps ? s.fps.toFixed(0) : '\u2014';\n      const ms = (s.frameP95 || s.frameP50 || 0).toFixed(1);\n      readout.textContent = `${fps} fps \u00b7 ${ms} ms`;\n    }, 500);\n  }\n}",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped a starter Performance panel. Open the playground, expand Settings \u2192 the new \"Performance\" subsection sits at the top.\n\n## What's in the panel\n\n**Live readout** in the header: FPS + frame p95 ms while the Perf overlay is enabled. When perf timing is off it shows \"enable Perf overlay \u2192\" because the underlying timestamping itself has a small cost, and we don't want the readout silently driving that on.\n\n**Quality preset** dropdown \u2014 moved here from below (kept its id so the existing wiring works unchanged). It's the master knob; the other toggles in the panel reflect what it does.\n\n**Four toggles** with two-way sync to their primary controls elsewhere:\n- WebGL render \u2014 proxies `toggle-webgl`\n- Cursor preview \u2014 **NEW**, the only knob with no other UI. Maps directly to `wc.cursorPreview()`.\n- Wet heatmap \u2014 proxies `toggle-wetness-heatmap`\n- Perf overlay \u2014 proxies `toggle-perf`\n\n**Footnote** pointing to the other perf-relevant knobs that live in their own homes (Resolution, Advection mode, Edge darkening, Fade).\n\n## Design choices I made and why\n\n**Mirror, don't duplicate logic.** The four shared toggles use a `MutationObserver` on the primary toggle's class list and a click-forwarder. This means: if you change the primary toggle from anywhere else (or it gets flipped by Quality preset selection), the mirror updates automatically. The opposite direction works too \u2014 clicking the perf-panel mirror clicks the primary, which runs its existing handler. **One source of truth, no drift.**\n\n**Cursor preview snaps Quality to Custom.** Because cursor preview is one of the things Quality preset controls (Minimum forces it off). Toggling it manually means the preset no longer matches, so we drop to \"\u2014 Custom \u2014\" like the existing Resolution slider does.\n\n**Mini readout polls the existing perfMetrics API.** No new lib API. Just reads what's there.\n\n## Honest scope check \u2014 what I deliberately did NOT do\n\n1. **No new perf knobs added to the lib.** Everything in the panel maps to existing lib state. The point was discoverability and grouping, not new capability.\n2. **No automatic perf-driven quality adjustment.** I considered adding an \"Auto\" mode that watches FPS and drops the Quality preset if frames go above 16ms. But that's a behavior change that could surprise users mid-stroke; it's a separate feature.\n3. **The mini readout requires Perf overlay to be enabled.** I didn't want the panel to silently turn on perf timing for everyone. Reading the value when it's off would just show 0. Better to be explicit.\n4. **I left existing controls in their existing locations.** Moving them would break muscle memory for users who know where things are. The panel anchors and re-presents perf-related ones, but doesn't replace them.\n\n## What you'd reasonably want to add next\n\nA few things I considered but didn't ship \u2014 flag if you want any of them:\n\n- **Auto-perf mode** \u2014 watches FPS and auto-drops Quality when frame time exceeds a target. Would need a debouncing strategy or it'd thrash.\n- **Pigment fade controls in the panel** \u2014 fade is in the toggles row but has perf cost (per-frame pass). Could surface its half-life slider here when on.\n- **Resolution slider mirror** \u2014 same pattern as the toggles. I didn't include it because resolution is the single biggest perf lever and deserves its own row, not a buried mirror. Cross-link in the help text instead.\n- **Live \"active rect\" indicator** \u2014 the perf overlay shows this; surfacing it in the mini-readout would let users see when their canvas is \"busy\" (large active rect = more cells iterated each step). Worth thinking about if you're tuning a busy scene.\n\nLet me know which of these (if any) are worth building, or if there's a specific perf knob I missed that you want surfaced.",

Let me think about this carefully before building anything, because the question hides several different scopes and the right answer depends on which one you want.\n\n## What you might mean by \"watercolor shader\"\n\nThree substantially different things, all called \"watercolor effect\":\n\n**1. Pure post-process filter.** Take an input image, run it through a fragment shader, output a watercolor-looking version. No simulation, no time, no interaction. The output is a single frame that looks watercolor-y. Classic image processing \u2014 edge detection, color quantization, paper-texture multiply, color bleeding via blur, dark-edge enhancement. **Cheap, instant, completely separate from the Washes lib.**\n\n2. **Simulation seeded from an image.** Take an input image, deposit its colors as initial pigment into the Washes simulation, then let the sim run \u2014 bleed, dry, evaporate. The watercolor \"look\" emerges from the physics over time. This is essentially `paintImage` (which already exists), just framed as a complete experience. **Reuses the existing sim, requires no new code.**\n\n3. **Pixel-level watercolor shader with time evolution but no pigment mixing.** A new GLSL shader (separate from Washes) that runs its own simplified per-pixel sim: each pixel has wet+pigment-as-RGB, advects with a velocity field, edge-darkens, bleeds, evaporates. Skips K-M math (your \"without worrying about pigmentation mixing\"). **New shader code, related to but separate from Washes.**\n\nEach is a real project. They serve different purposes:\n\n- #1 is what people typically mean by \"watercolor filter\" \u2014 Instagram-style effect on photos\n- #2 is \"use my image as a starting point for the simulator\"  \n- #3 is \"build a fluid sim that looks like watercolor but is faster/simpler than Washes\"\n\nWhen you said \"complementary shader\" and \"without worrying about pigmentation mixing\", I think you're describing **#3** \u2014 a sister tool to Washes that has the look but cheaper math. The \"deluge input\" part confirms this \u2014 you want simulation interactivity, not just a static filter.\n\nBut I want to push back gently on whether #3 is what you actually want.\n\n## Cost-benefit reality of #3\n\nWashes is ~9000 lines because watercolor is genuinely complicated when you want it to look real. K-M pigment math is the part that gives you *interesting color mixing* (rose+blue\u2192purple, wet pigment lifting and re-depositing, granulation). Stripping that out leaves... a fluid sim that pushes RGB colors around. Existing in many places \u2014 Pavel Dobryakov's WebGL fluid, jamie wong's, dozens of shadertoys.\n\nIf you skip K-M, you'd also skip:\n- Edge darkening (because that depends on wet-field gradient logic that's tied to the sim \u2014 though you could re-derive it from RGB diffusion)\n- Pigment evaporation transfer (suspended \u2192 deposited)\n- The granulation/staining variations that make different pigments behave differently\n\nSo #3 ends up being: \"a generic fluid sim with paper texture and edge darkening.\" It's not Washes-without-K-M; it's a different thing entirely.\n\n**Whereas #2 \u2014 just using `paintImage` to seed the existing sim \u2014 gets you 80% of the same experience for 0% new code.** You paint your image into Washes, then drag-deluge to your heart's content. Pigment math doesn't slow it down meaningfully; the perf cost is dominated by the advection/render pipeline, not by the K-M math.\n\nI want to surface this before building. **My honest recommendation: skip #3 unless there's a specific reason** (like you want to deploy a shader that's not coupled to the Washes runtime, or you need GPU-only with no CPU fallback, or you want a much smaller bundle size).\n\n## What might actually be useful here\n\nA few options I'd offer:\n\n**Option A \u2014 A demo page that uses Washes for the image effect.** Drop an image onto a `<div>`, the page calls `paintImage` to seed Washes with it at high paint load and high water (so the colors aren't crisp text but pooled wet pigment), pause the sim briefly, then a UI button \"Deluge\" splashes a wet wave across it. Same sim, new wrapper. Maybe 100 lines of HTML+JS, no new lib code.\n\n**Option B \u2014 A standalone \"watercolor filter\" shader.** Pure post-process #1. Static, no time, no deluge. ~80-line GLSL fragment shader that takes a texture and outputs a watercolor-ified version. Useful as a \"convert any image to watercolor\" tool. The deluge interaction wouldn't really fit \u2014 the shader has no concept of time.\n\n**Option C \u2014 A fluid sim shader (option 3 from above).** Standalone, ~300 lines of GLSL + JS scaffolding, simulates a wet field + RGB pigment field with advection and edge darkening. Independent of Washes. Could ship as `washes-fluid.html` or `washes-lite.html`. Real work, but creates something genuinely new.\n\n**Option D \u2014 Some hybrid I'm not seeing.**\n\n## Which one are you actually after?\n\nI genuinely can't tell from the question, and the choice substantially affects what I build. The phrasing \"complementary shader\" reads like Option C (a sister tool), but \"takes an image and makes it look like watercolor\" reads like Option A or B.     "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-14T17:40:57.048645Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Ah, now I understand the actual goal \u2014 and it's a really good one. Let me think hard about this before building.\n\n## The real use case\n\nYou want a thumbnail grid where each thumbnail is a watercolor-rendered JPG that bursts/disperses when clicked. Multi-instance perf is the constraint, which means **the cost-per-instance** matters more than absolute peak quality. With many thumbnails on a page, even Washes at its lowest quality preset would burn through frames because each instance has its own simStep + render pass.\n\nThe right framing isn't \"K-M shader vs simulation.\" It's: **how do I get the watercolor look on N thumbnails simultaneously without paying N \u00d7 (full Washes cost)?**\n\n## Several real approaches, ordered by complexity\n\n**1 \u2014 Lazy Washes instances.** Each thumbnail is a Washes instance, but they're paused (`wc.pause()`) by default. Click \u2192 resume + deluge \u2192 re-pause once settled. Cost is near-zero for idle thumbnails. Issue: paused canvases still consume DOM space, GL contexts (if WebGL), and memory. A page with 20 thumbnails = 20 GL contexts, which browsers may refuse to allocate (Chrome caps at ~16).\n\n**2 \u2014 Single shared Washes instance, render-to-texture per thumbnail.** One sim, but you swap state in/out per thumbnail and render to a texture displayed in the right grid slot. Issue: state-swap is expensive (copy 8+ Float32Arrays per thumbnail per frame). Defeats the purpose.\n\n**3 \u2014 Custom shader with K-M but no advection sim.** A fragment shader that, given an input image, applies the watercolor *look* (K-M reflectance, paper texture, edge darkening) per pixel without any time evolution. On click, drive an animated deluge displacement via a per-thumbnail uniform that gets ticked. This is **a real new thing** \u2014 purpose-built for static-input + simple-burst.\n\n**4 \u2014 CSS/SVG filter chain on JPG thumbnails.** No GLSL at all. Stack of CSS filters (saturate, blur, contrast, drop-shadow) + an SVG turbulence displacement map. Click triggers a CSS animation that drives the displacement parameters. Cheapest possible. Looks decent but not great \u2014 limited to filter primitives.\n\n**5 \u2014 Pre-rendered video / sprite-sheet.** Generate the deluge animation as a video or sprite-sheet at build time, play it on click. Zero runtime cost but no per-image customization, no live K-M math.\n\n## What you actually want, I think\n\nReading the question again: **\"K-M shader with deluge\"** specifically asks for the K-M part, which rules out option 4 and 5. **\"Improve performance on a page with multiple canvases\"** rules out option 1 (still has the per-canvas overhead). Option 2 is the wrong architecture.\n\nSo **option 3** is what you want \u2014 a focused shader that:\n- Samples a JPG as input\n- Applies K-M-style watercolor reflectance per pixel (no actual simulation, no advection)\n- Has a displacement function driven by a click-time uniform that creates the deluge effect over ~1-2 seconds\n- Renders to a small WebGL canvas per thumbnail\n\n## What this shader looks like, concretely\n\nA vertex shader passing UVs through, and a fragment shader doing roughly:\n\n```\ninput: uTexture (the JPG), uDelugeT (0..1, time since click), uDelugeCenter (where the click was)\n1. Compute displaced UV: original UV + radial displacement from uDelugeCenter, scaled by some easing function of uDelugeT\n2. Sample uTexture at displaced UV \u2014 gives you the \"stretched\" colors\n3. Convert sampled RGB \u2192 estimated K/S coefficients (since real JPG pixels don't have K/S, we approximate)\n4. Apply kmReflect() with a thickness driven by 1 - uDelugeT (deluge thins the pigment over time)\n5. Multiply by paper-texture noise for granulation\n6. Edge-darken based on local gradient (cheap proxy for the wet-field gradient that drives Washes' edge darkening)\n```\n\nStep 3 is the tricky part. Real K-M wants K and S per pixel; an RGB JPG only gives reflectance. The inverse mapping (R \u2192 K, S) is well-defined for opaque pigments under specific assumptions, but doesn't uniquely determine the pair. I'd use a simplification: assume `S = 1.0` everywhere (constant scattering) and derive K from the reflectance equation. This means colors render *approximately* as K-M would render them at full thickness, but pigment \"mixing\" doesn't happen because we have only one cell of data per pixel.\n\nThat's the \"**K-M shader without pigment mixing**\" \u2014 we get the K-M *appearance* (the way colors deepen and granulate against paper) but not the *interaction* of multiple pigments. For a static-input thumbnail effect, that's exactly the right tradeoff.\n\n## The deluge animation\n\nI'd model it as:\n- **Outward radial displacement** that ramps up over ~0.4s, then fades over ~1s\n- **Local pigment lift** (reduce thickness uniform smoothly across the deluge front)\n- **Color bleed** via increasing the displacement noise as the deluge passes\n- **Paper-revealing** at the click point as the deluge sweeps outward\n\nTunable uniforms: deluge speed, radius, intensity, lift amount. One animation frame per thumbnail, scheduled via rAF, only running for thumbnails with an active deluge \u2014 idle thumbnails render once and stay static.\n\n## Cost-per-instance estimate\n\nPer-thumbnail cost when idle: **zero** \u2014 render once to a static canvas, no rAF.\n\nPer-thumbnail cost during a deluge: one fragment shader pass at thumbnail resolution (say 256\u00d7256 = 65k pixels), ~30 instructions per pixel = ~2M GPU ops per frame. **Microsecond-range on any modern GPU.** Even 20 simultaneously delugin' thumbnails = trivial cost.\n\nThis is dramatically cheaper than 20 simultaneous Washes simulations. **It's the right architecture for the use case.**\n\n## A few honest caveats before building\n\n1. **The look won't match Washes exactly.** K-M without advection means no flow patterns, no granulation accumulation at edges, no wet-on-wet bleed shapes. The deluge is a procedural radial effect, not a fluid simulation. It'll look \"watercolor-y\" but not \"Washes-y.\"\n\n2. **JPG colors don't have K/S.** My approximation gives reasonable K-M-style rendering but isn't physically calibrated against any specific pigment. The colors deepen and granulate but a JPG photo of a person won't suddenly look like it was painted in quinacridone rose.\n\n3. **WebGL context limits still apply.** Browsers cap concurrent WebGL contexts at 16ish. For thumbnail grids exceeding that, you'd need to share one big WebGL canvas + draw all thumbnails into it as sub-viewports, or fall back to a 2D canvas filter. I'll structure the code so it's swappable, but the default will be one context per thumbnail (works fine up to ~12-15).\n\n4. **Mobile.** Fragment shaders run well on mobile GPUs but the JPG-decoded texture upload (which happens on click in the deluge case if you swap inputs) can stutter on low-end devices. For a thumbnail grid I'd preload textures at page load.\n\n## What I'll build\n\nLet me build option 3 as a standalone HTML file. Structure:\n\n- A `<class>` `WatercolorThumbnail` you instantiate per thumbnail with `new WatercolorThumbnail(canvas, imageUrl)`\n- It manages its own WebGL context, vertex/fragment shaders, texture upload\n- `.deluge(x, y, opts?)` triggers a burst from (x, y) (canvas-local coords)\n- `.reset()` returns to the static rendered state\n- Internal rAF is created only during active deluges; the canvas stays static otherwise\n- Click handling auto-wires if you pass `{ clickToDeluge: true }`\n\nI'll ship a demo page showing 6-9 thumbnails in a grid, each clickable. Independent file, doesn't touch the Washes lib. If you later want to share noise textures or paper textures across thumbnails I can refactor \u2014 for now keep it simple.\n\nOne more decision worth checking before I commit:",
